explicit_text: I remember where I was on Pearl Harbor Day very clearly. It was December 7, 1941, and I was in North Zulch visiting family for the weekend. My brother and I only found out about the attack when we returned to Conroe that Sunday afternoon around 4 PM from a filling station attendant. At first, we didn't even know where Pearl Harbor was, but once we learned, we realized how serious the situation was. My parents were very upset, especially since two of my older brothers were of draft age.

explicit_text: I attended Texas A&M University and completed four years of ROTC training there, graduating the spring before Pearl Harbor. Although I had finished all my training, I wasn't commissioned until the following spring because I was not yet 21 years old on December 7, 1941. Texas A&M was where I prepared for my military career, which became very relevant after the war started.

explicit_text: Before heading overseas, I was at Camp Stoneman, California, for about three weeks. This was a staging area before we embarked for Australia. Camp Stoneman was where we prepared to ship out as part of a large troop movement.

explicit_text: I arrived in Australia in July 1942 after an eight-day trip on the USS Monterey. We trained in Brisbane for about three weeks and then conducted maneuvers in the Outback on large cattle and sheep stations. The Australians were very friendly and reminded me a lot of Texans. I also encountered many New Zealanders training there, as most Australian men of draft age were already fighting in other theaters.

explicit_text: I traveled to Australia aboard the USS Monterey, which was a Matson liner converted to a troopship. There were about 6,000 of us on board, and the trip took about eight days, which was record time. The ship had peacetime crew and excellent food, and officers had separate quarters with two men per room. It was my first time on a ship, and many men got seasick during the voyage.

explicit_text: Brisbane, Australia, was where we arrived in July 1942 and where we trained for about three weeks. The city was very picturesque with several top-notch parks featuring native Australian animals like kangaroos and wallabies. We stayed in Brisbane for about six months before moving on to New Guinea. The Australians there were very welcoming, and the city left a strong impression on me.

explicit_text: I had applied for a two-week vacation to Sydney while stationed in Brisbane because I had heard a lot about the city and wanted to visit. However, the day I was supposed to leave for Sydney, we were loaded onto a ship to head to New Guinea instead. So unfortunately, I never got that chance to see Sydney during the war.

explicit_text: I was deployed to New Guinea after leaving Brisbane, traveling on a Liberty ship with about 200 men. We traveled in convoy with four destroyers escorting us from Townsville to Oro Bay on the north coast of New Guinea. We landed by unloading onto lighters since there were no docks at Oro Bay, and we were quartered right on the coastline. Malaria was a big problem there; almost all of us contracted it and had to take Atabrine throughout our time overseas.
